courses.name: Computer Programming I Laboratory
courses.purpose: This course is designed for 1st year computer engineering undergraduate students. The students will be introduced to fundamental concepts of computer programming using Java. This course covers basic programming concepts such as variables, data types, iteration, methods, arrays, etc.
courses.description: This course will begin with an introduction to computing and solving problems in a programmatic way. The discussion will then focus on the development of programs using the datatypes and control structures available in the Java programming language.
learning_outcomes: To be able to declare and assign values to variables using user-entered data, information from a file, or expressions
To be able to display information to an output visible to a user or data file
To be able to have a program choose a path or repeat a set of instructions based on a true or false condition
To be able to create a procedure for code reuse and call the procedure when necessary
To use an array of values and be able get information from the array or manipulate the values in the array
resources: Y. D. Liang, 'Introduction to Java Programming, Comprehensive Version' 10th edition, Pearson

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Science
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to explain fundamental concepts of computer science. The reason is to prepare students for development of scientific, engineering and industrial software applications, and also to equip students with knowledge and advanced skills in problem solving, modeling and scientific computation.
courses.description: This course will begin with a short introduction by giving information about the history of computer science. The following topics will be presented from slides in this course during the semester: The Role of Algorithms, The History of Computing, Theory of Computation, Computer Graphics, Image Processing, Digital Design, Operating Systems, Data Structures, Software Engineering, Database Systems, Networks and the Internet, Artificial Intelligence
learning_outcomes: One can think critically and creatively, conceptualizing real world problems from different perspectives.
One can explain how computing components may be combined to build computer systems.
One can explain how computer hardware represents information.
One can apply general problem-solving strategies to the development of computer algorithms
One can apply the object-oriented methodology to computer problem solving.
One can explain the role of an operating system in managing and interacting with computer system components
One can use information system software to organize, manage, and secure data.
One can explain how to use computer networks to share resources
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: The Role of Algorithms, The History of Computing
resources: Computer Science – An Overview, 12th edition, J. Glenn Brookshear, Dennis Brylow, Pearson, 2015, ISBN 10: 1-292-06116-2

courses.name: Career Planning
courses.purpose: The aim of the Career Planning course is to guide students to determine their careers in accordance with their own intelligence, personality, knowledge, skills, skills and competencies. Within the scope of the course; Awareness will be raised about these concepts, Career Centers and activities where students can get support about their careers throughout their university life will be introduced, talent gate usage and how to benefit will be shown and the opportunity to meet working life in different sectors will be offered.
courses.description: Within the framework of the draft created by the Presidential Office of Human Resources, it is recommended to process the career planning course with video and activities prepared for each week and guest trainers to be invited from university faculty members, industry professionals, non-governmental organizations and international organizations. The supportive activities to be included in the course are designed to inform the students about the methods and tools used in professional applications and to give them the ability to use them in the most effective way and are supported by practical activities. Career centers will follow the course practically with activities that provide experience opportunities to help students develop their skills. The recommended program on the structure and content of the course is presented below. The program may vary depending on the characteristics of the universities and the departments in which the students who will take the course are enrolled.
learning_outcomes: To raise awareness of students` interests, personal characteristics and values.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Your Career Journey Begins First Stop: Career Center
Hafta 4: Key to Making a Difference in the Career Path: Skills
Hafta 6: How to Prepare for My Career?
Hafta 14: Talent Gate I'm Writing My First Résumé
resources: Videos supporting course content recommended by the Presidential Office of Human Resources.

courses.name: Object-Oriented Analysis and Design
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to equip students with a solid foundation in object-oriented design principles and methodologies, enabling them to design, develop, and maintain flexible, scalable, and high-quality software systems that can adapt to changing requirements. Students will gain practical skills to analyze, model, and solve complex software design problems using modern tools and techniques.
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of object-oriented design, including key principles such as encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism. Students will learn how to gather and analyze software requirements and design flexible, scalable systems using principles like SOLID and DRY. The course also covers essential topics such as software architecture, design patterns, and managing changing requirements in dynamic environments. Emphasis is placed on iterative design and testing methodologies to refine and improve systems. Through case studies and real-world projects, students will gain hands-on experience in applying these principles and techniques to solve complex software design problems.
learning_outcomes: Master the foundational principles of object-oriented design, including encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.
Apply design principles like SOLID and DRY to create maintainable and scalable software systems.
Effectively handle evolving requirements and incorporate them into software designs.
Develop structured solutions for large-scale and complex software systems using object-oriented methodologies.
weekly_topics: Hafta 14: Case Studies and Real-World Applications
Hafta 15: Project Presentations and Course Review
resources: Head First Object-Oriented Analysis and Design by Brett McLaughlin, Gary Pollice, David West

courses.name: Digital Design
courses.purpose: This course provides a modern introduction to logic design and the basic building blocks used in digital systems, in particular, digital computers. It aims to familiarize students with the fundamental principles of digital design and classical hardware design for both combinational and sequential logic circuits.
courses.description: The course covers combinational logic, including logic gates, minimization techniques, arithmetic circuits, and modern logic devices such as field programmable logic gates. It also covers sequential circuits such as flip-flops, synthesis of sequential circuits, counters, registers, random access memories, and state machines. Different representations including truth tables, logic gate diagrams, timing diagrams, switch representations, and state diagrams are discussed.
learning_outcomes: Ability to practice arithmetic computations in binary numbering system.
Ability to explain the basic operations and theorems of Boolean algebra.
Ability to apply rules of Boolean algebra to simplify Boolean expressions.
Ability to explain how to translate Boolean expressions into equivalent truth tables and logic gate implementations.
Ability to explain the fundamentals of logic design from the gate up to the system level.
Ability to design efficient combinational and sequential logic circuit implementations from functional description of digital systems.
Ability to practice simple simulations to verify the operation of logic circuits.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Number Systems and Binary Numbers
Hafta 3: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates
Hafta 4: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates
Hafta 7: HDL, Verilog and Gate Delays
resources: M. Morris Mano, Digital Design, 5th edition, Prentice-Hall Inc.

courses.name: Microcontroller Programming
courses.purpose: To introduce students to the architecture and operation of typical microprocessors and microcontrollers. To familiarize the students with the programming and interfacing of microprocessors and microcontrollers. To provide a strong foundation for designing real-world applications using microprocessors and microcontrollers.
learning_outcomes: Explain the general construction of a microcomputer system. Explain that a microcomputer system consists of a CPU, RAM, ROM memories, input /output interfaces, and peripheral units. Explains how to make receiving and sending of data. Explains the terms ‘address and data bus. ’Examine the units in the microcomputer system. Draw a simple memory schema, and explain the planning of memory of a microcomputer system. Examine the construction of CPU, know registers, and bus systems. Show how to run instructions in a sample microcomputer system ( Fetch, decode, execute ).

Compare microprocessors and microcontrollers. Know the structural differences between microprocessors and microcontrollers. Express that the classical type of microprocessors consists of not only CPU but they consist of I/O, timer, etc., interface units. Express that microcontrollers are the only integrated construction equipped by the processor, I/O, timer ADC / DAC, and serial communication unit. Express that microprocessors and microcontrollers are used as controlling units in similar industrial applications, and program logic has a grand similarity.

Explain to set up microprocessors and microcontrollers system. Set up the connection between the processor and a very important part, the oscillator part, of a microprocessor/microcontroller and explain how to work it. Set up the connections between RESET and power parts of the system and Express their working. Explain the duties of control lines and, if necessary, set up the connection. Express that processor will program with hex. cod. Show their knowledge and ability of software which will be used for programming of process in assembly or advanced level.

Explain the information of introduction to programming. Explain the arranging of the typical assembly language program and its structure. courses.name: Advanced Application Development
courses.purpose: This course offers a deep dive into web development, emphasizing both client-side and server-side technologies. Starting with the fundamentals of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, students will learn to create responsive and interactive web pages. The course progresses to introduce server-side programming with a focus on Spring MVC, a powerful framework for building web applications in Java.
courses.description: Advanced Application Development course is crafted to introduce students to the essentials of creating websites, focusing on using Spring MVC for the server-side part. Starting with basic web development skills like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, it teaches how to make websites look good and work well. The course then dives into Java and Spring, showing how to build web applications that can handle data and respond to users smartly. Through 14 weeks, students will learn to set up web projects quickly with Spring Boot, connect their applications to databases using Spring Data JPA, and create web services that other programs can talk to. The course also covers how to make web pages update without reloading using AJAX, keep applications safe with Spring Security, and test everything to make sure it works as expected. By the end, students will work on a project that brings all these pieces together, showing off what they've learned.

Recognizes and uses Telephony and SMS.

Recognizes and uses SQL databases

courses.name: Fundamentals of Cloud Computing
courses.purpose: Describes techniques to write programs in cloud environments and to create software architecture suitable for these environments.
courses.description: Cloud computing is a scalable services consumption and delivery platform that provides on-demand computing service for shared pool of resources, namely servers, storage, networking, software, database, applications etc., over the Internet. It is a model for enabling ubiquitous, on-demand access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources, which can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort. This course will introduce various aspects of cloud computing, including fundamentals, management issues, security challenges and future research trends.
learning_outcomes: will have knowledge about cloud management.
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Resource management in cloud environments
Hafta 5: Service Management in Cloud Computing
Hafta 6: Data Management in Cloud Computing
Hafta 8: Open Source and Commercial Clouds, Cloud Simulator
Hafta 9: Research trend in Cloud Computing, Fog Computing
resources: John Rhoton, Cloud Computing Explained: Implementation Handbook for Enterprises, Recursive Press, 2010.

courses.name: Internet of Things
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to provide students with a foundational understanding of the Internet of Things (IoT), covering the core concepts, technologies, and applications that underpin IoT systems. Through a combination of theoretical knowledge and practical experiences using the Arduino ESP32 platform, students will learn to design, implement, and secure IoT solutions applicable to consumer, industrial, and smart city contexts. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to critically assess IoT technologies and their impact on society, as well as to innovate within the field by developing their own IoT projects that demonstrate an understanding of connectivity, data handling, and device management within an IoT ecosystem.
courses.description: This introductory Internet of Things (IoT) course encompasses a comprehensive exploration of IoT fundamentals, including the architecture, components, and operation of IoT systems. Through engaging lectures and hands-on labs using the Arduino ESP32 platform, students will delve into the intricacies of sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, connectivity options, and IoT protocols such as MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP/HTTPS. The course also covers IoT platforms and tools for device management and data analytics, alongside crucial considerations for IoT security, privacy, and ethical design. By integrating theoretical knowledge with practical projects, the course aims to equip students with the skills to design, implement, and secure innovative IoT solutions across various applications, preparing them for advanced studies or careers in the burgeoning field of IoT.
learning_outcomes: Understand the Fundamentals of IoT
Master IoT Communication Protocols
Develop and Implement IoT Solutions
Utilize IoT Platforms and Tools
Address IoT Security and Privacy Concerns
Analyze and Visualize IoT Data
Critically Evaluate IoT Applications
Innovate with IoT Technologies
weekly_topics: Hafta 7: IoT Data Handling and Storage
Hafta 12: Smart Home, Consumer IoT, and Industrial IoT
Hafta 14: Course Wrap-up and Project Presentations

courses.name: Introduction to Image Processing
courses.purpose: This course provides an introduction to image analysis and computer vision for undergraduates. We will start with low-level vision (early processing) techniques such as binary image analysis, filtering, edge detection and texture analysis. Then, we will cover mid-level vision topics such as image segmentation and feature extraction in detail. Finally, we will do case studies on several applications such as image classification, object recognition, and deep learning.
courses.description: Image acquisition, sampling and quantization. Spatial domain processing. Image enhancement. Texture analysis. Edge detection. Frequency domain processing. Color image processing. Mathematical morphology. Image segmentation and region representations. Statistical and structural scene descriptions. Applications.
learning_outcomes: Apply principles and techniques of digital image processing in applications related to digital imaging system design and analysis.
Analyze and implement image processing algorithms
Gain hands-on experience in using software tools for processing digital images.
resources: Gonzalez, R., Digital Image Processing, Prentice Hall, USA, 2002

courses.name: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: This is an introductory course in Artificial Intelligence. The goal is to acquire knowledge of intelligent systems and agents, formalization of knowledge, reasoning with and without uncertainty, machine learning, and applications at a basic level.
courses.description: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence. Problem-solving using search. Predicate logic. Planning. Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence. Machine learning. Reinforcement learning.
learning_outcomes: Distinguishes between a conventional system and an intelligent system. Describes data, information, and knowledge. Explains algorithmic versus heuristic methods. Defines structured and unstructured data.
Explains the Artificial Intelligence concept and its applications. Knows how to represent knowledge. Describes searching methods. Explains approximate reasoning.
Represents knowledge using various different techniques. Explains first-order logic and predicate calculus. Explains semantic network. Explains a rule-based system. Explains case-based system.

Uses the appropriate searching technique to achieve desired goals. Knows state space search. Distinguishes between data-driven and goal-driven search. Uses depth-first search and breadth-first search. Uses some heuristic search methods.
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Solving Problems by Uninformed Search
Hafta 4: Solving Problems by Informed Search
resources: Stuart J. Russell and Peter Norvig, Artificial Intelligence - A Modern Approach, 3rd Edition, Prentice Hall, 2009.

courses.name: Computer Graphics
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to explain the key ideas, concepts and methods in computer graphics area, and to teach how to apply these methods in projects.
courses.description: This course will begin with an introduction including the human visual system and history of graphics softwares and cover the course topics presented in the syllabus. The lectures will be mainly based on presentation of lecture slides and discussions. All lecture slides will be made available on the course web page. In the lectures, programming examples will be presented using computer by projecting computer screen on the board. Programming homeworks will be given regularly. To analysis and understanding the applications developed for the homeworks, documentations including source codes will be required. An important part of the course is the term project. By the first month of the semester, each student will have chosen a topic for his/her project. Projects will be based on developing applications, which are 3 dimensional modelling oriented or one of the computer graphics topics. Depending on the chosen topic, students will do a literature survey to solve the specified problem and then they will design and implement their applications using C/C++/OpenGL, Java3D or a similar graphics API. Students are expected to submit a project proposal by the end of the first month of the semester. There will be 2 written exams, which aim to test students' understanding of concepts in computer graphics field raised in lectures.
learning_outcomes: One can understand the structure of modern computer graphics systems
One can develop an application based on computer graphics and understand the basic principles
One can know and develop key algorithms for modelling and rendering graphical data
One can gain experience in constructing interactive computer graphics programs using OpenGL
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Homogeneous Coordinates, Aspect Ratio, Line Generation Algorithms (e.g. DDA, Bresenham algorithm)
Hafta 6: Lighting, Shading ve Local Shading Models, Texture Mapping
Hafta 7: Ambient, Diffuse and Specular Reflections
Hafta 10: Geometric Objects and Transformations (scaling, rotation, translation)

courses.description: Language evaluation criteria. Describing syntax and semantics. Tools for constructing lexical and syntactical analyzers. Names, bindings, type checking, and scopes. Data types. Expressions and the assignment statement. Statement-level control structures. Subprograms. Abstract data types. Concurrency. Exception handling. Functional programming languages. Logic programming languages.
learning_outcomes: Distinguish the most appropriate programming language for developing a program (Analysis).
Explain the functions of system programs and the interactions among them throughout the program development process (Comprehension)
Recognize programming language concepts and constructs by having an opinion on their realizations (Knowledge).
Use a new programming language by understanding the concepts- not syntax- supported (Application).
State similarities and differences between evolutionary development of popular programming paradigms (Knowledge).
Appraise the different roles played throughout the life cycle of a program (Analysis)
weekly_topics: Hafta 9: Expressions and Assignment Statement, Statement-Level Control Structures
Hafta 12: Concurrency, Exception Handling and Event Handling
resources: Sebesta R. W., Concepts of Programming Languages, 10th Ed. (2012), Pearson, 2004.

courses.name: Microservice Architecture
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of Microservices Architecture, a modern approach to software design and development. Students will explore the principles, patterns, and best practices of microservices, along with hands-on experience in designing, implementing, and deploying microservices-based applications. By the end of the course, students should be equipped with the knowledge and skills necessary to architect scalable, resilient, and maintainable systems using microservices.
courses.description: The course content for the Microservices Architecture course is designed to offer a deep dive into the intricacies of modern software development practices. It starts by exploring the foundational concepts of microservices, highlighting their advantages over traditional architectures. Students will progress to mastering containerization technologies like Docker and orchestration tools such as Kubernetes, essential for managing complex microservices environments efficiently. Throughout the course, students will engage in hands-on projects to implement communication patterns between microservices, ensuring seamless interaction within distributed systems. They'll also study crucial aspects like data management strategies, security considerations, and advanced topics like event-driven architecture and serverless computing. Practical sessions on monitoring, logging, and debugging will equip students with essential skills to maintain the robustness and reliability of microservices-based applications. By the end of the course, through a comprehensive final project, students will showcase their proficiency in integrating these technologies to develop scalable and resilient microservices architectures.
learning_outcomes: Design and implement microservices architectures that promote scalability, resilience, and maintainability.
Utilize containerization and orchestration technologies such as Docker and Kubernetes to deploy and manage microservices-based applications.
Apply communication patterns and data management strategies to facilitate effective interaction between microservices
Implement security measures to protect microservices-based systems from unauthorized access and data breaches
Analyze and troubleshoot microservices architectures using monitoring, logging, and debugging techniques
weekly_topics: Hafta 9: Service Discovery and Load Balancing
Hafta 11: API Gateway and Circuit Breaker Pattern
Hafta 13: Resilience and Fault Tolerance, Deployment Strategies

courses.name: Parallel Computing
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to develop parallel algorithms, to develop algorithms on different parallel computer topologies to gain the ability of parallel programming. From embedded devices, laptops, to high-end supercomputer, and large-scale data centers, parallel computing is widely employed to achieve performance and efficiency targets. This course introduces the foundations of parallel computing, including parallel architectures, parallel programming methods and techniques, parallel algorithm designs, and parallel performance analysis. It is an introduction to parallel programming, how to parallelize programs, and how to use basic tools like MPI and POSIX threads.
courses.description: Analysis of parallel algorithms. Real and apparent parallelism. Parallel programming and parallel programming compilers. Message Passing Interface. Scheduling and performance analysis. Parallel computer topologies and applications with the hypercube architecture.
learning_outcomes: will learn to write software applications suatible to parallel architectures.
Define terminology commonly used in parallel computing, such as efficiency and speedup.
Describe different parallel architectures, inter-connect networks, programming models, and algorithms for common operations such as matrix-vector multiplication.
Given a problem, develop an efficient parallel algorithm to solve it.
Given a parallel algorithm, analyze its time complexity as a function of the problem size and number of processors.
Given a parallel algorithm, an input to it, and the number of processors, show the steps performed by that algorithm on that input.
Given a parallel algorithm, implement it using MPI, OpenMP, pthreads, or a combination of MPI and OpenMP.
Given a parallel code, analyze its performance, determine computational bottlenecks, and optimize the performance of the code.
Given a parallel code, debug it and fix the errors.
Able to develop, analyze, and implement algorithms for parallel computers. This applies both to computers with shared memory and with distributed memory.
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Principles of Parallel Algorithm Design.
Hafta 6: Analytical Modeling of Parallel Programs
Hafta 7: Programming Using the Message-Passing Paradigm
Hafta 8: Programming Shared Address Space Platforms
resources: Introduction to Parallel Computing, Second Edition, Addison Wesley

courses.name: Social Network Analysis
courses.purpose: The aim is to teach the scope, definition, and applications of Social Network Analysis (SNA) both theoretically and practically. Accordingly, it involves understanding how individuals/actors within a social network search for and share information, particularly in areas of interest to SNA and specifically in agriculture. This includes determining the influence and degree of impact of actors, as well as visualizing and explaining complex networks related to individuals through mathematical and statistical methods.
courses.description: Social network analysis is used to analyze institutions, individuals, groups, or any structures interacting within a social framework to generate information by making inferences about an individual or a group. Therefore, the social network analysis course covers topics such as network theory and graph theory, network metrics, social network theory, social network analysis (SNA), the importance and applications of social network analysis, its practical applications and use in agriculture, agricultural information and knowledge systems, social capital and social organizations, data collection techniques for social networks, traditional methods and online data collection, and the use of widely adopted software packages for social network analysis.
learning_outcomes: 1. To know the development process of Social Network Analysis, its conceptual framework (introduction to networks, i.e., nodes, edges, networks, node degree, connected components, diameter, preferential attachment, network centrality), and the importance and methods of network analysis.
2. To examine the actors in the field of study individually and as a community through network theory.
3. To analyze institutions, people, groups, or any structures that are in relation with each other within a social structure.
4. To make inferences and interpretations about the areas of influence of an individual or a group through their interactions.

5. To understand the basic concepts of social network analysis, including introduction to networks, i.e., nodes, edges, adjacency matrix, one- and two-mode networks, node degree, connected components, average shortest path, diameter, preferential attachment, and network centrality.

courses.name: Large Language Models
courses.purpose: Large Language Models (LLMs) are one of the most important technologies of the information age, with applications across many language-based domains such as web search, advertising, emails, customer service, language translation, virtual agents, medical reports, and politics. This course introduces students to cutting-edge research in deep learning for NLP and LLMs, and helps them develop the skills needed to design, implement, and understand neural network models using the Pytorch framework.
courses.description: This course provides a holistic view of the current state of large language models. It begins with the basics of language models, including network architectures, training, inference, and evaluation. It then covers interpretation, alignment, emergent capabilities, and popular applications in language tasks and beyond text. The course also discusses scaling up pretraining, efficient training and deployment of large models, deployment concerns, and the challenges and frontiers of LLM development.
learning_outcomes: Compare and contrast different models in the LLM ecosystem in order to determine the best model for any given task.
Implement and train a neural language model from scratch in Pytorch.
Understand how to apply LLMs to a variety of downstream applications, and how decisions made during pre-training affect suitability for these tasks.
Research recent academic papers related to BDMs and learn common terms used in these papers such as alignment, RLHF, prompt engineering, and instruction tuning.
Design new methodologies to leverage existing large scale language models in novel ways.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Large Language Models
Hafta 3: Backpropagation and Neural Network Basics
Hafta 8: Conversational AI and Virtual Assistant
Hafta 12: Building a Custom LLM App with Langchain
Hafta 13: Question-Answering Application on Custom Documents
resources: Natural Language Processing with Transformers — O’Reilly
Practical Natural Language Processing — O’Reilly

courses.name: Engineering Economics
courses.purpose: To improve the ability of economic thought and understanding of an engineer in the decision-making process of engineering economics.
courses.description: Engineering Economics is an application of economic principles in the analysis of engineering decisions. The course covers principles of economics and an introduction to engineering economics, with emphasis on economic issues, decision-making, and the application of economic concepts to engineering problems.
learning_outcomes: Inflation and escalation
Cost
Engineering economics decisions, cost, profit maximization, the concepts of equivalence is aimed to teach
Economic Equivalence
Break-even point
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Economics: Meaning and Characteristics. Scarcity How people make decisions: Trade-off, efficiency, equity, opportunity cost, marginal changes, marginal cost, marginal benefit, incentives.

Hafta 2: Consumption and production. Factors of production: Labor, capital, government, entrepreneurship. Sectors of economy: Household sector, business sector, government sector, international institutions. Free market. Open economy, closed economy. (Turkish economy and other economies as an example)

Hafta 3: How people interact with one another: Trade, competition in the market, benefits of trade. Market economy, invisible hand, government intervention to the markets. Capitalist economy, command economy, mixed economy.
Hafta 4: Market failure. Externalities. Market power. Demand and Supply. Demand curve and supply curve. Market equilibrium, demand surplus, supply surplus.
Hafta 5: How the economy as a whole works: A country's standard of living The reasons of differences in living standards among countries Productivity What to make to increase the productivity
Hafta 6: Money supply in the market Prices when the government prints too much money Inflation Unemployment Philips curve The relation between inflation and unemployment

Hafta 7: The reasons of inflation Interest rates Exchange rates Goverment incomes, government expenditures, budget deficit. The effects of increasing butget deficits in an economy. The effects of the expectations of investors.

Hafta 8: Open market transactions Central Bank of Turkish Republic. Inputs and outputs: The decision making process of what outputs to produce, and in what quantity,how to produce them,for whom to produce. The formulations of total productivity, labor productivity, capital productivity.

Hafta 9: The production possibility frontier The difference between productivity and efficiency. Efficiency and inefficiency. The law of diminishing returns. Perfet competition, imperfect competition. Capacity utilization rate.

Hafta 10: How markets solve the basic economic problems. Failure of Invisible hand. Monetary policy and fiscal policy. Trade, money and capital. The effects of larger stock of capital to the economic growth. Economic fluctuations. Three functions of money: medium of exchange, measure of economic value, medium of saving. Specialization and division of labor.

Hafta 11: International finance Euro and dollar as the key instruments in international trade. International financial centers and their properties. Capital flows. The effects of euro and dollar to Turkish economy.

Hafta 12: Engineering decision making process: Setting design goals and objectives. Evaluating design alternatives. Gauging product cost and price. What makes economic decisions differ from other design decisions?
Hafta 13: The engineers role in business. Making capital-expenditure decisions. Large-scale engineering economic decisions. Types of strategic engineering economic desicions.

Hafta 14: The nature of decision problems that a typical engineer might face in the real world: service or quality improvement, new products or product expansion, equipment and process selection, cost reduction, equipment replacement. Fundamental principles of engineering economics.
